Sure. Consider [haydavies](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/28cc9fb975b4c0b5f4bc471a1f151ab612293044/pvlib/irradiance.py#L697):

```python
# a bunch of stuff that can handle any input type
# but this line throws a TypeError for scalar input
sky_diffuse[sky_diffuse < 0] = 0

# instead, use
sky_diffuse = np.maximum(sky_diffuse, 0)
```

Similar for reindl, king, and beam_component.

[haurwitz](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/blob/28cc9fb975b4c0b5f4bc471a1f151ab612293044/pvlib/clearsky.py#L275) has a couple of problems:

```python
# works with any input
clearsky_GHI = 1098.0 * cos_zenith * np.exp(-0.059/cos_zenith)

# throws a TypeError for scalar input
clearsky_GHI[clearsky_GHI < 0] = 0

# output is promoted to DataFrame whether you like it or not
# throws a ValueError for array input with 2 or more dimensions (including DataFrame input)
df_out = pd.DataFrame({'ghi':clearsky_GHI})
return df_out
```

I suggest the following makes more sense for the low level functions:

```python
# works with any input
clearsky_GHI = 1098.0 * cos_zenith * np.exp(-0.059/cos_zenith)

# works with any input
clearsky_GHI = np.maximum(clearsky_GHI, 0)

# output type = input type
return clearsky_GHI
```

The DataFrame wrapper could still be applied in the Location.get_clearsky method that enforces pandas timeseries. I only added the DataFrame wrapper to haurwitz so that it and the ineichen function would have a consistent return type. 

I suspect that the vast majority of pvlib analyses use 1-D array-like inputs, but it can be very convenient for functions to accept scalar inputs when you want to better understand what they do to your data.

Finding an example made me realize that I was very sloppy in my lists. relativeairmass, physicaliam, and ashraeiam set the out of bounds data to nan, so they belong on the slightly harder list. snlinverter has one line that is easy to fix, but a second line that is not.

Here's part of snlinverter with more comments:

```python
    # promote scalars to a pandas series so that we can use indexing
    # Exception if p_dc has more than 1 dimension
    p_dc = pd.Series(p_dc)

    ac_power = (Paco/(A-B) - C*(A-B)) * (p_dc-B) + C*((p_dc-B)**2)
    # easily replaced with np.minimum
    ac_power[ac_power > Paco] = Paco
    # but this is more complicated
    ac_power[p_dc < Pso] = - 1.0 * abs(Pnt)

    # enables scalar in, scalar out
    # but disables one element array/series in/out!
    if len(ac_power) == 1:
        ac_power = ac_power.ix[0]

    return ac_power
```

I think this would be better:

```python
    ac_power = (Paco/(A-B) - C*(A-B)) * (p_dc-B) + C*((p_dc-B)**2)
    ac_power = np.minimum(ac_power, Paco)

    if np.isscalar(p_dc):
        ac_power = -1.0*abs(Pnt) if p_dc < Pso else ac_power
    else:
        ac_power[p_dc < Pso] = - 1.0 * abs(Pnt)

    return ac_power
```

I'm not 100% sure about the best way to handle these cases. np.isscalar is probably preferable to try/except because exception handling is somewhere between hard and impossible in numba and cython (last I looked, at least).

In response to the first scenario, it still requires one of the 2 values to take precedence over the other so there is the situation where one of us changes the value of the WD flag and causes a name space collision which we can just avoid all together with the second scheme, and in general the running of a script should be independent of platform to prevent these side effects.

As to the second, the conversion scripts which translate magnetometer data to MagIC data do not use ipmag functions for the main body of their work they do it locally. They used to parse data independently in the main function into variables according to a boolean which defined which of the 2 values (command line or kwarg) should be used. This resulted in a lot of redundant code and a unnecessary kwarg. So to make things more pythonic and standardized as well as shorter I moved things into the standard python script structure. Now if this doesn't work I'll have to group them as suggested above. Putting them in main would again result in the same problem which value do you use and either redundant code or the possibility for future collisions? For the conversion scripts this is a very real question as Pmag GUI has a -WD flag and so do the conversion scripts, and they have a WD kwarg.

The 2 function solution separates operation from input and doesn't add more than a line to the code for a new function declaration. It also allows us to make it universal in PmagPy that main is just the command line input function and should not be called from the python interpreter or other scripts avoiding all possible name space problems and mutations.

To answer your question about 'How can i figure out if i am getting v1 content or v2 content, and in case i get v2 content which schema version i got pulled'

There are specs for each version. 

this is how v1 content looks like https://github.com/moby/moby/blob/master/image/spec/v1.md#image-json-description

With regards to v2 content, there was introduced a concept of mediatype which basically tells you manifest schema1 or schema2 you pulled( omitting the manifest list for now, since we dot support that yet)

v2 schema1 version https://docs.docker.com/registry/spec/manifest-v2-1/
v2 schema2 version https://docs.docker.com/registry/spec/manifest-v2-2/

To make a curl request to crane API you need to simulate docker client's behaviour( because as you pointed out when you do `docker pull` you don not know what you pulled)

It is important what kind of docker client you have, based on this you curl request will be different.

For example older docker clients to not set anything in the accept headers, newer ones, set the mediatype they accept( i.e. manifest list, schemav2 and schemav1)

```
$ curl -L -H "Accept: application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v1+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json, application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.list.v2+json" -k -X GET 'http://localhost:1234/v2/foo/manifests/latest'
{
   "schemaVersion": 2,
   "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.distribution.manifest.v2+json",
   "config": {
      "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.container.image.v1+json",
      "size": 1465,
      "digest": "sha256:00f017a8c2a6e1fe2ffd05c281f27d069d2a99323a8cd514dd35f228ba26d2ff"
   },
   "layers": [
      {
         "mediaType": "application/vnd.docker.image.rootfs.diff.tar.gzip",
         "size": 677607,
         "digest": "sha256:7520415ce76232cdd62ecc345cea5ea44f5b6b144dc62351f2cd2b08382532a3"
      }
   ]
```

I spent some time digging into this to see if I could find a fix. 

Them memory leak seems to be exclusive to when an `Executor` has to reshape to a size larger than the previously binded size. When you size down there is no noticeable memory leak.  I have updated my git repo that as the bug reproduction to show this. 

This could be related to the new empty `NDArrays` being created here https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/master/scala-package/core/src/main/scala/org/apache/mxnet/Executor.scala#L105 and then the previously binded arrays that they are replacing are not properly being freed somewhere. I am assuming the problem is avoided in the resize to a smaller size case since the backend implementation of `reshape` on the `NDArray` has no problem reusing the old native memory space for the `NDarrays` since it can fit the new size in the same memory space as the old size. I tired disposing the old `NDArrays` for the old size of the `Executor` in various places but could not find a place that worked (or any proof that disposing the old `NDArrays` wold fix the memory leak)

I looked into the python API to see if it was doing anything different that could be adapted into the Scala interface to solve the problem. It looks like the Python API has changed from resizing the `Executor` in Python to using a new backend function to resize the `Executor`. (Link to the PR for that https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/pull/10882). I figured that this new backend implementation avoids the memory leak issue I made a Jira ticket for the same change (https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/MXNET-747) and attempted to make the same change in the Scala interface (https://github.com/jessebrizzi/incubator-mxnet/tree/MXNET-747), but I am having trouble writing the new JNI interface for the native resize call and I could use some eyes on it that are more familiar with the JNI interface for the Scala API (getting a SIGSEGV on the call to the `MXExecutorReshape` method here https://github.com/jessebrizzi/incubator-mxnet/blob/MXNET-747/scala-package/native/src/main/native/org_apache_mxnet_native_c_api.cc#L1764 and I'm not sure why). 

Any help or eyes on this problem would be greatly appreciated, I am currently using MXNet in a production environment and have a pretty hacky work around in place currently to avoid this issue.

Also, a mistake one might make, so for reference I am adding it here.
The shape inference may not always work with a chained composition such as:
  ```
discouragedSelfMatches <- mx.symbol.broadcast_mul(lhs = mx.symbol.broadcast_equal(lhs = distances,
                                                                                    rhs = mx.symbol.min(distances),
                                                    rhs = mx.symbol.max(distances))
```
but it will work if the symbols are separated, for instance:
  ```
selfMatches <- mx.symbol.broadcast_equal(lhs = distances,
                                           rhs = mx.symbol.min(distances))
  discouragedSelfMatches <- mx.symbol.broadcast_mul(lhs=selfMatches, 
                                                      rhs=mx.symbol.max(distances))
```

The ONNX specification recently changed the definition of the BatchNorm operator, see here [opset_version7](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Operators.md#BatchNormalization) and [op_setversion6](https://github.com/onnx/onnx/blob/master/docs/Changelog.md#BatchNormalization-6).

So currently there are many models that use opset_version6 and some that use opset_version7. This error is caused due to this discrepancy in the operator definition. I will try to prioritize and get a patch fix to handle both versions of the operator and merge it into the master.

Actually I was wrong about the specific cause of this bug. In C++ a const reference extends the lifetime of a temporary. However in this case (line 1230):
1. The ^ operator is evaluated, producing a temporary IfcVector3
2. Normalize() is called on this temporary. The lifetime of the temporary is extended to the lifetime of the reference. This is NOT the local variable face_nor but instead the implicit "this" parameter of Normalize().
3. Normalize() runs and returns a reference to "this". This would be safe if "this" were a normal value which in this case it isn't.
4. The reference is stored in loal reference variable face_nor. The extended lifetime of the temporary ends. The reference now points to a destroyed object. Referencing it is undefined behavior.
5. Since the lifetime of the reference ends on line 1237 and the only other variable in this scope is a float this will work fine most of the time. However on GCC with -O2 (but not -O1 or -O0) the optimizer will do Bad Things and generate bad code.

TL;DR: It was a real bug and this was the correct fix but not for the reasons I though it was. And do not EVER use this feature of C++, it's fraught with peril. Just store locals by value and avoid getting your legs blown off. All code in the project should be audited and this idiom removed.

What does your `celery.py` file look like? Since `djcelery` has gone away, you'll never be starting Celery through `manage.py` anymore. Instead, you start it directly from the `celery` command. For this to work though, Django will have to be initialized, which means you need the `DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE` environment variable.

Your `celery.py` file should contain this at the top, before you create your `app`:
```
import os
os.environ.setdefault('DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE', 'myproject.settings')
```

I'd like to use `$HOME` everywhere, but `~` sometimes slips in when I'm not paying attention.
I agree that `~` is more idiomatic / "comfortable", but it's not quite as safe.

The reasoning is simply that shells respect `"~"` by not expanding, so, if someone copies a dirname and quotes it, bad times ensue.
I'll amend to have no `~`s.
I'm going to close this but feel free to re-open if there's more to discuss.

@jagerman In a4381aa I've collapsed most of py3 enum impl into the `type_caster<>` which gets rid of the unnecessary map lookup on cast/load. Also, `py3_enum_info` is templated on the underlying type which gets rid of all `long long`.

As get_include is being rewritten, perhaps this is also an opportunity to get rid of the `user=bool` switch?  AFAICT, the currently recommended setuptools scheme is to pass both `get_include(user=True)` and `get_include(user=False)` to `include_dirs`.  This means that if two different versions of pybind11 are installed system-wide and at user-level, the first listed will win; so `user=True` should probably be listed first, to follow the normal Python import path.  But this then means that the PYTHONNOUSERSITE environment variable would not be respected in that case.

Instead, I would suggest making `get_include()` check whether `pybind11.__file__` is a child of `site.USER_SITE`; if it is, then behave as `get_include(user=True)` did, if not, behave as `get_include(user=False)` did.  This may optionally be combined with deprecation of the `user=` kwarg (but that's not necessary either).

> Even so for the rectifier, why is there a need for division? Isn't the grad of the rectifier something like `x > 0 *dL/dx`?

Yes, but the rectifier is formulated as `0.5 * (x + abs(x))` because that makes both the forward and the backward pass more efficient: https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/pull/163#issuecomment-81806482
And then, the unoptimized gradient is:
```python
>>> import theano
>>> import theano.tensor as T
>>> x = T.scalar('x')
>>> y = 0.5 * (x + abs(x))
>>> z = T.scalar('z')
>>> g = theano.grad(None, known_grads={y: z})
>>> theano.printing.pp(g)
'((z * TensorConstant{0.5}) + (((z * TensorConstant{0.5}) * x) / |x|))'
```

For `x == 0`, this is undefined, but there is an optimization fixing this. Let's have a look at the optimized graph when compiling this into a function:
```
>>> theano.printing.debugprint(theano.function([x, z], g))
HostFromGpu [@A] ''   3
 |GpuElemwise{Composite{((i0 * i1) + (i0 * i1 * sgn(i2)))}}[(0, 1)] [@B] ''   2
   |CudaNdarrayConstant{0.5} [@C]
   |GpuFromHost [@D] ''   1
   | |z [@E]
   |GpuFromHost [@F] ''   0
     |x [@G]
```

So it seems the NaN you get here is caused by the NanGuardMode blocking some optimization (or did you disable some optimizations somewhere yourself?). It's not the NaN you were originally hunting for.

        i_t = self.inner_activation(xi_t + T.dot(h_tm1 * B_U[0], u_i))
        f_t = self.inner_activation(xf_t + T.dot(h_tm1 * B_U[1], u_f))

Restarting from `EARLIEST` can actually result in repeated messages, since it sets the consumer offset to the earliest offset available for each partition. This means that if you've consumed some messages on a partition and then the consumer resets its offsets, it will go back to the earliest offset still held on that partition - probably one of the ones you just consumed. Neither `EARLIEST` nor `LATEST` will actually guarantee that the consumer will pick up where it left off after a failure - the best way to ensure this is to commit offsets regularly, as you're doing.

The 'all'/'any' modes can be easily implemented by using 'exact' and checking if the answer is =1 (for all) or >0 (for any).

Also had to learn by trying that the hooks are for the active renewal process only, and that pre/post-hook just allow the authenticator/installer to work (e.g. change www config, stop/start some services).

As a simple solution for --after-all (besides using a wrapper script in the cron tab) I touch a file when a certificate has been renewed and a separate hourly cron job will reload Apache or Postfix if necessary.
As renewals are done several days before the old certificate expires I find that an hourly cron job is fast enough. 
Additionally I'm missing that the renew-hook is executed also on the certonly command (see #3947). 

/etc/cron.d/certbot
```
0 */12 * * * root test -x /usr/local/bin/certbot-auto && perl -e 'sleep int(rand(3600))' && certbot-auto -q renew --renew-hook /usr/local/bin/letsencrypt_renew-hook.sh
```

/usr/local/bin/letsencrypt_renew-hook.sh
```
#!/bin/sh

#{
#  echo "--- $(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S) ${0}"
#  env | grep -e 'RENEWED'
#  echo "----------------------------------------"
#} >>/tmp/letsencrypt.renew-hook.txt


[ -z "${RENEWED_DOMAINS}" ] || {
  touch "${RENEWED_LINEAGE}/renewed"

  ### Workaround that CertBot does not save renew-hook in renewal configuration file
  ## Get first/main domain of certificate
  for DOMAIN in ${RENEWED_DOMAINS}
   do
    break
  done

  ## Act on main domain
  [ -z "${DOMAIN}" ] || {
    case "${DOMAIN}" in
     ### www.example1.net
     (www.example1.net)
      /usr/local/bin/letsencrypt_renew_www.example1.net.sh
      break
      ;;
     ### www.example2.com
     (www.example2.com)
      /usr/local/bin/letsencrypt_renew_www.example2.com.sh
      break
      ;;
    esac
  }
}

exit 0
```

/etc/cron.hourly/letsencrypt_reload_apache2 (important: no dots in script name)
```#!/bin/sh
BASEDIR='/etc/letsencrypt/live'
CERTDOMAINS='www.example1.net www.example2.com'

[ -d "${BASEDIR}" ] || exit 0
[ -x /usr/sbin/apache2 ] || exit 0
[ -n "${CERTDOMAINS}" ] || exit 0

### Check if Apache has to reload configuration incl. certificates
RELOAD=0
#
for DOMAIN in ${CERTDOMAINS}
 do
  CERTDIR="${BASEDIR}/${DOMAIN}"
  [ -f "${CERTDIR}/renewed" ] || continue
  RELOAD=1
  rm "${CERTDIR}/renewed"
done
#
[ "${RELOAD:-0}" -eq 0 ] || /bin/systemctl reload apache2

exit 0
```

In order to prevent snapshots from being incomplete (i.e. one of the nodes isn't able to write to the shared filesystem), Curator runs a [repository verification](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/modules-snapshots.html#_repository_verification) before each run if you have a supported version of Elasticsearch (1.4+).

What this suggests is that adding the VPC endpoint has altered Elasticsearch's ability to write and/or verify snapshots, or it's too slow to respond, resulting in a false positive (or negative, as the case may be).  With the VPC enabled, you should probably run the API call manually and see what shakes out, both in the returned message, and in the Elasticsearch log files:

    curl -XPOST localhost:9200/_snapshot/REPOSITORY_NAME/_verify?pretty

Because this is a safety feature, I do not recommend trying to work around it without knowing for sure that all of your nodes are truly backing up properly.  That said, if this VPC endpoint is timing out, you can use the [`--skip-repo-validation`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/skip-repo-validation.html) flag, if you're using 3.2.0

Yeah, I was avoiding mentioning `emitter`, because I wanted to avoid the discussion about the subtle differences between signals and traits. Where signals are state-less, traits have state, so a richer payload is warranted IMO. If you're already creating a dict for the payload, might as well include the owner. 

As long as you stay `<=` 5 items in the dict, you don't pay any extra allocation costs and the items will fit in the inlined small table. Once you hit 6 items, you'll get a rehash and a secondary heap allocation, which can have a big impact on dispatch performance:

```python
>>> import sys
>>> a = dict(zip(range(4), range(4)))
>>> b = dict(zip(range(5), range(5)))
>>> c = dict(zip(range(6), range(6)))
>>> sys.getsizeof(a)
280
>>> sys.getsizeof(b)
280
>>> sys.getsizeof(c)
1048
```

I stumbled over a problem or at least a risk when the enum implementation is merged.
The following uses of ``traitlets.Enum`` are at least problematic (or no longer valid):

```python
# -- FILE: traitlets/config/application.py:156
# CASE: Mixed numbers and string
# BUT: Easily solvable (in traitlets), but may break compatibility of existing use cases.
  # OLD: 
  log_level = Enum((0,10,20,30,40,50,'DEBUG','INFO','WARN','ERROR','CRITICAL'),
  # NEW:
  log_level = Enum([('NOTSET', 0), ('DEBUG', 10), ('INFO', 20), ('WARN', 30), ('ERROR', 40), ('CRITICAL', 50)], ...)

# -- FILE: IPython/core/interactiveshell.py:182
# CASE: Only numbers
autocall = Enum((0,1,2), default_value=0, config=True, help="...")

```
And, in general, if there is an error in collection or any of these other steps, that nbgrader should fail more gracefully.

My concern is the following scenario:

You are running Jupyter modified to include the knowl Javascript, so that knowl `<a>` elements are automatically set up. I (with my evil hat on) write a knowl containing some Javascript, and put it on a public web-server configured to allow cross-origin requests. I make an interesting notebook which references my knowl, and send it to you. You open it up, start reading, and click on the knowl link. My evil Javascript gets to run in your notebook page, where it can send code to the kernel connected to the notebook. I can now take control of your computer.

I haven't tested this all the way through, but I have tested that Javascript in a knowl runs, and it attempts to load knowls from other domains. Even if it didn't load from other domains, I think there are still ways to exploit it - you're unlikely to look carefully at an HTML file buried in a git repository you've just cloned, for instance.

Putting the content in a sandboxed iframe would mean that the Javascript runs in a separate context from the notebook, so it can't talk to your kernel. This isn't entirely specific to Jupyter notebooks: it could matter on any page where the Javascript has access to some private resources (e.g. a login token) and knowls might come from an untrusted source.

You can probably set a permanent override in `~/.jupyter/custom/custom.css`. You might need a `!important` on it to force it to take precedence over other rules. The `!important` tag is a blunt instrument in CSS terms, so it might mess something else up a bit.

I'm not sure what the best default is here. For wrappable text, it's generally agreed that you want to limit the maximum width, because it's hard to read very long lines. E.g. Github issues is only taking ~1/3 the width of my screen for this comment, even on a laptop. But I can see that for things like large tables this is frustrating.

 - There is a bug in zoom/pan which resets the view periodically.  This makes navigating trees a pain if it doesn't fit in the window.
 - Up to a few 100 leaf nodes D3 is passable.  Draw speed is ok.  All nodes at a given depth are rendered at the same x-coord which makes wide trees tall and thin.

It would be useful to know what constitutes a large tree for others.

This PR could be changed to use the old visualisation but supporting both would require refactoring and add complexity.  Right now, I'm finding the D3 vis useful so I'd like to find a way to make it work.  I'll look more closely at the code to consider options.

Well, having a ``setup_requires`` is inherently more fragile with more broken edge cases than not having a ``setup_requires`` (much in the way that having an ``install_requires`` is inherently more fragile with more broken edge cases than not having an ``install_requires``). The top issues are (from memory):

* ``setup_requires`` suffers from the terrible problem of needing to delay imports some how (e.g. no ``import incremental`` at top level).
* ``setup_requires`` is downloaded and installed by setuptools not pip, so there's often confusion that comes in the edges where setuptools and pip have different behavior. An example is ``pip install -i https://example.com/index/ klein`` would still try to pull incremental from PyPI unless you added extra config files to point setuptools away from PyPI and towards your custom repo.
* ``setup_requires`` will be invoked for *all* invocations of ``setup.py``, even ones that don't need it like ``setup.py clean`` unless you go out of your way (like pyca/cryptography did) to fix that.

That being said, a lot of the problems with having a ``setup_requires`` become way less important, particularly for pure Python packages, since we now have Wheels and ``setup_requires`` simply does not apply and in all likelihood most people installing a pure Python project with a wheel in 2016 are going to be installing the wheel not the sdist. This makes the above edge cases far less of a big deal and tips the scale in the favor of using the mechanisms for abstraction in ``setup.py``, flawed as the mechanism may be, since they'll be used by an increasingly smaller subset of people.

That being said, similarly to ``install_requires``, it's largely a person choice of how much benefit you're gaining from whatever is in the ``setup_requires`` and whether the "cost" of the ``setup_requires`` outweighs the benefits or not. My own metric tends to be that for your average project, once you start getting above trivial code (like a ``with open(): read()``) the math pretty quickly favors using ``setup_requires``, especially for pure Python programs, unless you have some evidence or reason to believe that installation via sdist is a significant part of your user base.

It looks like reports are used in two different contexts. In one, they're like a report template, where you have some default settings and a name and an ID and things like that. In another, they're used to represent an instance of a running (or previously ran) report, which has unique runtime settings and a name and an ID. There are two cases (non-exclusive) when an object needs to be re-built:
1. the source file was changed
2. one of the files the source file depends on changed

An additional dependency, added by changing the corresponding source file, is trivial to detect (category 1).

When the header file or an indirect include was changed, this will be noticed via the modification dates of those files and the dependency file will be re-generated and the object re-built.
This should cover the case you described, right?

The same applies to `test_create_document`, `test_fetch_document`, `test_save_document` each of which is testing 2 or 3 cases. Ok so it seems that the problem is in ``rrule.weekday`` that for some reason has: ``SU(+1)`` being in fact ``SU(+1.0)``, so not an int but a float and thus the error.

We could do an easy fix by ensure at: https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/rrule.py#L65 that ``n`` is an int, but I'm strill trying to figure out why it becoming an int.

@jbrockmendel Yes, that would have been my preferred solution, but this would effectively be a backport of [`datetime.timestamp`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.timestamp), which treats naive times as local. It would also do the inverse of what `fromtimestamp()` does:

```python
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> from dateutil import tz
>>> dt = datetime(2018, 1, 3, 12, 20, tzinfo=tz.tzlocal())
>>> (dt.astimezone(tz.tzutc()) - datetime(1970, 1, 1, tzinfo=tz.tzutc())).total_seconds()
1515000000.0
>>> datetime.fromtimestamp(1515000000.0)
datetime.datetime(2018, 1, 3, 12, 20)
>>> datetime.utcfromtimestamp(1515000000.0)
datetime.datetime(2018, 1, 3, 17, 20)
```

I think I'm going to put this decision off for a release after 2.7.0 because I'm not quite sure what we should do about "compatibility" methods like this. It might be that we add a new `dateutil.compat` module that backports functionality from the latest version of CPython so that you can write your code in a version-independent way.

Finally all tests are passing and I have verified for python 3.7 on my machine. Please provide feedback on the implementation.

> The sample matrix X is a numpy array with n rows and 2 columns. The first column corresponds to the observation values, and the second column corresponds to the length of each observation.

X is a NumPy array (or an array like object) with `n_samples` rows and `n_features` columns. 

For the stock prices example `n_features` is indeed 2: 
* the first column contains the differences of the consecutive *close* values (described [here](parse_yahoo_historical)), and 
* the second column contains *volume* values.

It is possible to train an HMM over multiple sequences by passing a second argument `lengths` to the `.fit` or `.predict` methods. This is the format implemented in a similar [`seqlearn`](https://github.com/larsmans/seqlearn) library by @larsmans. 

I'll try to illustrate it with an example. Consider the following two 1D sequences

```
X1 = [1, 2, 0, 1, 1]
X2 = [42, 42]
```

To pass both of the to the `.fit` method we need to a) concatenate them into a single array and b) compute an array of lengths

```
X = np.append(X1, X2)
lengths = [len(X1), len(X2)]
```

Hope this clears things up a bit.

Thanks for the feedback and sorry for taking a while to respond.

I don't officially support Python 3.3 in any of my projects (I decided to focus on Python 2.6, Python 2.7 and Python >= 3.4 to keep compatibility manageable) however the issue you pointed out seems to be inherent in any Python 3.x version: The semantics of `map()` changed from returning a list to returning an iterator. The code in the last line of the traceback you quoted tests the iterator for its truth value which doesn't make any sense - this is a bug in pip-accel, I should have never written that code the way I did. I'm honestly not sure how I didn't notice that before. This is now fixed in pip-accel 0.29.3.

I'm closing this issue now because I'm confident this issue is now resolved, but if pip-accel 0.29.3 doesn't fix the issue for you feel free to reopen this issue or report a new one. Thanks again for the feedback!

This is a part of #224

Until recently the b2 API required that the hash of each uploaded part (large files are uploaded as 100MB parts) is sent before the content of this part. This requires reading each part twice and with the default setting of 10 threads the b2 cli will read about 1GB of data before actually uploading anything. Fortunately the API was extended to allow sending hashes at the end. I already implemented this in b2 CLI, so that each part is only read once, but this change has not made it to a release version yet.

Your broken pipe problems sound like a bad connection. The b2 CLI does include a resume functionality but it can only resume individual parts, not half uploaded parts. Again with 10 threads this means that you possibly need to reupload 1GB of data. You could try reducing the number of threads which may also help with slower connections.

You mentioned that a few gigabytes were already uploaded successfully. In this case there should be some parts that have finished and your upload should resume. Are you sure that files didn't change between uploads? B2 CLI uses the modified time to detect new files. This is a Flask-related issue.
I figured out a way that works with Flask and uWSGI:

```
app = Flask(__name__)
app.wsgi_app = WSGIApplication(APP_INSIGHTS_KEY, app.wsgi_app) # for request info tracking

@postfork
def worker_setup():
    uwsgi.atexit = worker_deinit
    app.tc = TelemetryClient(APP_INSIGHTS_KEY)

def worker_deinit():
    app.tc.flush()
    print("Goodbye", uwsgi.worker_id())

@app.errorhandler(Exception)
def exception_handler(e):
    app.tc.track_exception(*sys.exc_info(), properties={ 'foo': 'bar' }, measurements={ 'x': 42 })
    app.tc.flush()
    return make_response("Unexpected Exception", 500)
```
Note that this only works when debug is set to False in Flask.
Hope this helps
References:

#### Creating an extension:

An extension is a set of variables and parameters that can be added to a tax benefit system, but which purpose is too specific to be added to the general shared code base of this tax benefit system (e.g. Paris social prestations in `openfisca-france`)

An extension is loaded this way:

```py
tbs = SomeTaxBenefitSystem()
tbs.load_extension('path/to/extensions/folder')
```

The extension folder must have a specific architecture:
```
    extension_folder/parameters.xml # Optional parameters file. Must be called `parameters.xml
    extension_folder/{some_formula}.py # File containing formulas
    extension_folder/{other_formula}.py
    extension_folder/{some_formula}.yaml # Optional test files
    extension_folder/{other_formula}.yaml
```

@alex88 Restarting tmux is normally not needed. In case powerline bindings were updated you only need `powerline-config tmux setup` (except when there are *huge* updates). But automatic configuration reloading performed by daemon has two flaws:

1. Python modules used by powerline are not automatically reloaded on update. (Module reloading is in any case dangerous operation.)
2. New configuration files do not get automatically loaded (only changes to existing files do).
3. Sometimes changes to existing files also fail to load.

We want to enable `resource.py` inheriting concrete subclasses to optionally support arbitrary tmsh command execution.

We don't want to generally support that functionality because we prefer more RESTful approaches to configuration, however there are many tasks that require it.

When a URI requires `command` execution the concrete resource subclass should mixin the `CommandExecutionMixin`.

The `CommandExecutionMixin` should:

1.  Have a single method
2.  That method `exec_cmd` should have this logic:  https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/pull/356/files#diff-3e61f22b80d7378f3015da1c1b201798R58   EXCEPT:
2a.   Instead of always running `run` it should have a positional parameter:  `command`
2b.   Remove the _kwadd helper function.
2c.    Add the "valid commands" check like this:  https://github.com/F5Networks/f5-common-python/issues/357#issuecomment-216585631

The `class CommandExecutionMixin(object)` should be defined in `mixins.py`.

Telethon probably crashed when trying to receive a request before, I assume. So Telegram just keeps sending the rest of that old request which you couldn't fully receive. And since Telethon doesn't know it was receiving a request before, it cannot carry on, and attempts to read a _new_ request (starting with its constructor ID) which, obviously, doesn't match.

A possible workaround would maybe read until there is no more data left. Not the best option though.

I don't use `.create_new_connection()`. Both clients are created with constructor of `TelegramClient()`.
As I use same session for both clients, I entered Telegram code once.

The problem here as I understood not such about updates but about two processes working with same session name (and thus session file). Update handler seem to update "sequence" value storing previous value. So it doesn't update actual value from session file, but stored beforehand.

What is in general proper approach to connect to same Telegram user from two processes, threads? Should each work with different session names (and thus files)?
This proposal looks good to me. It conflicts with current behaviour so it would be a breaking change, but I think this is useful enough to justify it.

I removed the warning about empty coefficient dictionaries as suggested.

I now raise a `DeprecationWarning` if any dictionary entries are `None` or have an invalid key name.  This will become a `ValueError` after the deprecation period.

The list of slices return is slightly ugly imho. Coefficients in a single array is nice, but I wonder if it's possible to make that more self-describing somehow. Otherwise it's overall not really better than the dicts returned from other functions.

Yes, getAllUsers will get all users for a subscription (even though there is a keyword arg for workspace in the method signature).  What do you (or intend to do) with the results from the call to getAllUsers?  It is possible that your intended use would utilize less attributes than what are requested internally in the field fetch list or don't utilize reference attribute sub fields.  In that case there may be another approach that might be more successful for you.

First of all, +1 for providing all the details! Very good. So the issue here is this:

You are using Ratchet (or Thruway over Ratchet?) behind Nginx, and Ratchet announces ("Received utf-8 encoded message here from server.") roles it is _not_ supposed to announce, namely:

* subscriber, publisher, caller, callee

A WAMP _router_ must only announce "broker" and "dealer" (the router roles).

A WAMP _client_ must only announce "subscriber, publisher, caller, callee".

AutobahnPython detects the invalid roles (invalid roles for a WAMP router) and bails out.

IOW: this is a bug in Ratchet ..

For some reason this code is called right at the start of Ganga in loading the LCG plugin. This strikes me as it would be prone to failure if there was some problem with the proxy/grid setup. I suppose we'll get a better fix for this when the credentials branch is mainlined.

Firstly the command ```GridProxy,chooseCommandSet()``` is called numerous times throughout the running of the class despite this looking like an initialization decision for the proxy. (Again this will be deprecated so I can skip the fact that I find this a bit confusing)

When ```isType(..., ICommandSet)``` is first called the test of is this a voms or grid Proxy is performed.
The first time around the value of ```self.voms``` is ```''``` which returns False as it's empty but then this becomes 'dteam' on the gangaat@lxplus account which forces the ```if self.voms``` function to change it's behaviour once the ```self.commands``` have been changed to ```gridCommand```.

As ```if self.voms``` is always True this was repeated many times when using the above type check and the statement ```if self.voms:``` returned True because something had been initialized there. This causes the gridProxy to change to become a VomsProxy.

When ```not isType(..., ICommandSet)``` the test for a voms or grid Proxy is never performed as this variable has been initialized to a 'raw' ```ICommandSet``` object. This causes horrible errors to be thrown.

The correct solution I come to is: ```if not isType(self.command, (GridCommand, VomsCommand)):``` this leads to the self.command only being initialized once to the correct command set.


As for AFSToken I think this is working as appropriate as this is entirely within the __init__ function and is therefore only always called once per instance and I would argue that this particular check here is defunct.

Conclusion. I personally dislike tests to the equivalent of ```if someObject:``` especially when we're working in an environment which allows for Schema defaults to be changed. It's not very implicit and it just causes confusion when the object changes in an unexpected way imo. I know it's very PEP8 in a lot of situations but this doesn't become very readable when debugging imo.
I would argue it's _much_ better to have had ```if self.voms == '':``` which would have removed the problems we see here as well.

The wrong start point does not make the outline interpolation incompatible, it only produces an unappealing result. The wrong number of points or outlines, different types of points are what makes outlines incompatible. Within ANY interpolation, all interpolated points are between points on masters.  If they are not in between, it is not interpolation. I am afraid to say it is already 30 years ago that I wrote an editor for Icarus outlines, which included interpolation. The math is not complicated.

If we interpolate a large square with start point top left, with a small square with start point bottom right, we may get a shape that is smaller than the small square. But it is impossible to get a shape that is larger than the large square. But in this case of u111EF, we effectively get a "larger square". You would need to extrapolate for that.

If you get an interpolation where interpolated points are NOT between points on masters, where the result is quite different from that GlyphsApp, which is doing the right thing, then there is something going on that needs to be explained. What that something is, I don't know. I assume it has to do with not being able to manage 7 masters.  For fonts that have been designed with GlyphsApp and tested with fonts generated with GlyphsApp, you MUST get the same results using "fontmake". One of these two reasons alone justify a serious investigation into what exactly causes the "larger square". And I bet it is a bug.

Indeed, apparently pip just needed some time to catch up. However, when upgrading to glyphsLib 1.7.0, the [fontmake tests are failing because of a crash in varLib](https://travis-ci.org/googlei18n/fontmake/jobs/223860046), so this needs more debugging.

I think ufo2ft would be a better place to do the renaming, since this shouldn’t be Glyphs-specific. I’m building a font that uses an Adobe font where production names are stored in `GlyphOrderAndAliasDB` which I want to parse and set `public.postscriotName` based on it.

@DuaneEllis-TI, thanks for digging into this issue.

Looking at the ncp.txt log file, it seems to start off with one link-local IPv6 address:
```
110.303 | [INFO]-MAC-----: Sent IPv6 UDP msg, len:94, chksum:9fbf, to:4fe3a7e6a12761db, sec:no, prio:high
110.312 | [INFO]-MAC-----: src: fe80:0:0:0:9878:db1c:96b0:79d5
110.318 | [INFO]-MAC-----: dst: fe80:0:0:0:4de3:a7e6:a127:61db
```

But then switches to using a different one later on:
```
116.081 | [INFO]-MAC-----: Sent IPv6 UDP msg, len:92, chksum:18da, to:4fe3a7e6a12761db, sec:no, prio:high
116.090 | [INFO]-MAC-----: src: fe80:0:0:0:c742:9fc9:f6fe:7264
116.096 | [INFO]-MAC-----: dst: fe80:0:0:0:4de3:a7e6:a127:61db
```

The `MeshForwarder` component [selects the MAC Source Address](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/thread/mesh_forwarder.cpp#L422) by first doing a match of the IPv6 Source IID with the Extended Address and, if they do not match, uses the short 16-bit address for the MAC Source Address.

The `JoinerRouter` component  [does not specify an IPv6 source address](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/meshcop/joiner_router.cpp#L285), relying on [default IPv6 source address selection](https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/src/core/net/ip6.cpp#L1000).  However, it is not yet clear to me why it starts selecting a different source address.

Thinking about this a bit further the idea of mutating the call history for historic callbacks is not correct.
There are few reasons for this: 

- a `hookimpl` is marked as historic then it shouldn't be used as a callback that is re-invoked often and furthermore the full history *should* be available to any consuming plugins
- The intention for historic hooks (really it's somewhat of a hack) is to resolve causality dilemmas to do with late dynamic plugin registration - if you're (re-)registering plugins after another historic call you might be doing something wrong...
- For *reconnection handling* or other such multiple hook call situations a plain old `hookimpl` should be used (i.e the non-historic kind)

@luca-s 
>`portfolo_returns = (r1 + r2 + r3) / 3.` 
If I understand correctly, that means r1, r2, r3 are realized at the same time. That is, all these three sub-portfolios are simultaneously bought 24 periods ago and sold at the same period. This is not consistent with "having N algorithms each rebalancing every N periods".

Under an algorithm holding N sub-portfolios and reblancing every N periods, for a given period, only one of the N sub-portfolios is rebalanced, whose N-period return is realized, while others are not realized until they are rebalanced. In other words, for a given period, each of the past N-period return corresponds to a different time span. I don't know if it is appropriate to average over them.

In terms of the code, each element of `ret` corresponds to a different interval, for instance, ret[1] corresponds to periods from 1 to 3, ret[2] corresponds to periods from 2 to 4......
```Python
 lambda ret, period: ((np.nanmean(ret) + 1)**(1. / period)) - 1,
```
I do agree that there is an error between `oneP_portfolio` and `oneP_avg`. But I think only things like `oneP_r1, oneP_r2, oneP_r3` can be addable since they can be seen as realized at the same time, while `r1, r2, r3` may not be directly addable because they are not simultaneously realized and covers different time interval, not, at least, without some additional assumption.

I've been thinking about this issue and figured out that Betelgeuse user may want to add their business logic when providing the final value for a given token. With that in mind I think if Betelgeuse provides a way to specify a Python module where it can inspect and check for functions like `get_<token_name>` in order to get information for a given token that will allow users to do whatever they want to do in order to have the final token value.

For example, a new option to betelgeuse command group would be added so a user can specify the Python path for the tokens getter module:

```console
betelgeuse --tokens-getter-module 'my_package.tokens_getter'
```

The path to module must be available at the Python path so Betelgeuse will be able to import it.

Then an example tokens_getter.py module would be:

```python
"""Tokens getter

Helpers for getting tokens value
"""

def get_title(docstring, tokens, invalid_tokens):
    return u'{prefix} {title}'.format(prefix='MY_PREFIX', title=tokens.get('title'))
```

The benefits of having that kind of feature is that each Betelgeuse user will have the power of customization and business logic specific to the user's project will be handled by them.

The API for the `get_<token_name>` functions should be better defined so all the information the user can have is available to them.

Lambda freezes the function instance after the handler returns - you are guaranteed execution of code inside the handler, but threads, for instance, are not guaranteed to run. The timeline may look something like this

0ms: function starts
20ms: libhoney.Event.send() called
30ms: function exits - function frozen
100ms: batch send thread runs <--- this never gets to run

For a busy function, the timeline can look something like:

0ms: function invocation 1
20ms: libhoney.Event.send() called
30ms: function exits - function frozen
40ms: function invocation 2
50ms: libhoney.Event.send() called
70ms: function exits - function frozen
80ms: function invocation 3
100ms: batch send thread runs, queue empty
105ms: libhoney.Event.send() called <-- never gets sent
120ms: function exits - function frozen

You can of course tweak the batch send interval but that doesn't really solve the problem, so the flush is needed to ensure the queue is sent before returning.

@anselor Thanks for doing all the hard work to get these three branches to merge. My review and testing of the merged code looks good. I'm going to merge ply into master.

I guess our `Task` objects are probably on the heavy side for µpython currently, especially with the duplication of cancel stacks into children.

Looking at the uasyncio readme, it also seems like we'd need somewhat different timekeeping strategies, though our existing pluggable `Clock` interface might be enough to handle a lot of this.

I guess we'd also need a different IO backend. I have no idea what kind of `select`-like API is available on µpython.

The queue usage was supposed to address the ordering of the messages but I now realize the problem still remains once the queue becomes full; ie you're back to the `StrictFIFOLock` plus fair scheduler requirement. 

In terms of the serializer idea, maybe that's too magical and restrictive like you said. Perhaps something like this would do the trick:
```python
async with stream.sending_FIFO_lock as stream_sender:
    serialized = await seralize(data)
    await stream_sender.send(serialized)
```
Yes that's an extra line required in userland but honestly, the developer really needs to be educated as to what can go wrong when they have a multiple-tasks-sending-to-the-same-stream problem. IMO it's hard for me to think of a design where a single sender 'agent' would not be in the picture, especially with stateful communications. So we're probably trying to handle a case that may not be very common, and hence we can afford an extra line in order to be more explicit to the user about what's really going on here.

For higher level abstractions and connections that are more built to have multiple senders like http/2, this isn't really a problem because the API will be built in such a way that these problems will be solved in the library (either via sans-IO or otherwise).

Ok, the 2 people who would know this off the top of their head are out this week. But, others in the group have walked me through how this works and we have an idea that I think should get this working for you. It appears that the only thing missing, for you to define what you are trying to do in the grid option set mapping wizard (in your picture), is the app name  in the pulldown. We see that if we define an application in SMC and set the isGridCapable keyword in it's properties, then that application will show up in the pulldown so that you can select it and specify your grid option set mappings.

So, if you're up for trying this out, it should just be 2 simple steps, which you can easily undo and won't cause any problem if it doesn't just work. For reference, here's a link to doc regarding this:
http://support.sas.com/documentation/cdl/en/gridref/67371/HTML/default/viewer.htm#n1inymfs0b7go2n147xdknz0ygpx.htm

The part in that doc is at the bottom, named 'Specifying That an Application Is Grid Capable'. The list of applications in the dropdown comes from the application having the 'isGridCapable' keyword set in it's properties. As there is no SASPY application in the metadata, we can simple define one and set this property.

1) in the 'Folders' view in SMC (like in the picture in the doc link), there's a list of applications. The doc has the  SAS Addin for Microsoft Office selected. 
If you right click on Applications, you can selct 'New'->'Folder.
2) Do that and set the name of the folder to SASPy. Then right click and select Properties - just like in the picture in the doc. Then add a Keyword-> 'isGridCapable' and save it. Just like the picture in the doc.

After creating the SASPy folder (application), and setting it to grid capable, when you go back in to the grid options mapping wizard, SASPy should now be available to choose and you can set this up as you want to.

This is as far as I can go here, so I can't prove that it will just work when you try running this, but I really do expect it will. You may need to restart something, I don't know. When you created other Grid Option Set Mappings, do they take effect dynamically or do you need to restart the object spawner or something? Same protocol, whatever that is.

If there is still something else that needs to happen and this doesn't just work as expected, you can simple delete the mapping you created and the SASPy folder, and you should be back to where you were before. And, next week I can track down any other disconnect with the developers who know this best.

I think having the appname set by saspy (like in the log recors I showed), and you being able to specify which app server (in the saspy config def), should get you everything you need here. 

Looking at the doc, it looks like if the new folder you create is named 'SASPy' (exactly the same as what saspy is setting for it's appname=) then you just need to set 'isGridCapable'. If you name that folder differently, say 'TomSaysThisShouldWork', then you would need to set the keyword property to 'isGridCapable: TomSaysThisShouldWork'.  That seems like the only other piece that makes this all match up and work.

Sorry I can't really validate that here, but again, this should be a very simple thing you can try and see, and easily back out if it doesn't work. Let me know if you have any questions, and whether it works or not. If so, I'll document it in the saspy doc so others can get this configured too!

Thanks,
Tom

There is an example for what you do in the documentation - specifically the [OpenSearch example](https://sentinelsat.readthedocs.io/en/v0.11/api.html#opensearch-example).

The docstring of `query` also lists the valid parameters. In your case you need to change `beginposition` to `initial_date`. Internally we format the search to `[<intitial_date> TO <end_date>]`. Therefore your query needs to look like this:

```python
products = api.query(footprint,
                     initial_date = 'NOW-10DAY',
                     end_date='NOW',
                     platformname = 'Sentinel-2')
```

Community alerts have been moved to one embed per alert

The logic for determining what streams appear in the embed is as follows:

1. Get the list of streams currently live on a community (100 of them)
2. Any streams with a stream alert in the current channel (i.e. ones created with `[p]streamalert twitch channel`) are automatically excluded from the possible selections as those streams will already have an alert posted
3. `random.sample` is used to get 10 to display in the embed

At this time, communities are checked every 60 seconds, which will result in the embed updating every 60 seconds. It may be worth discussing reducing the check frequency on communities though

wrote:

> I should add: Currently, TPOT uses a *generational* evolutionary
> algorithm (EA) model. That means that the EA keeps some fixed population of
> pipelines (by default, 100) and has to evaluate all of them before
> proceeding to the next generation. From an engineering perspective,
> generational EAs can be problematic because there can be a single pipeline
> that takes forever to evaluate and that will hold up the whole process.
>
> For the longest time, we have wanted to re-engineer TPOT to follow a *steady
> state* EA. With a steady state EA, pipelines are evaluated in parallel
> and the "parents" of the new pipelines are chosen based on the current
> population of pipelines that are already evaluated. That way, if one
> pipeline takes forever to evaluate, the EA can keep on optimizing based on
> the results from the faster pipelines.
>
> The diagram below sort of communicates those differences, if you stare at
> it long enough.
>
> [image: image]
> <https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1719223/42972661-084d073c-8b65-11e8-9692-c54c68ff2002.png>
>
> Just something to keep in mind as this branch is worked on. I suspect that
> dask would seamlessly support this huge upgrade to TPOT.

I have a first draft of this in pyqode 2.7.dev. Unfortunately I could not integrate the text cursor position changes into the editor's undo stack. The problem is that I don't have a direct access to the undo stack which is buried down into the private implementation of the Qt base class I use for the editor. So I had to maintain a second undo stack myself and push a new command anytime the cursor's line number changed (and you're not using jump back/jump forward).

So basically you have the following shortcuts:
- Ctrl+Alt+Z: jump back to previous cursor location
- Ctrl+Alt+Y: jump forward to the next cursor location (after one or more jump back). 

If you want to try it, add ``@dev`` at the end of each pip command in your dev script ([see](https://github.com/OpenCobolIDE/OpenCobolIDE/blob/dev/.travis.yml#L46))

The next version of both OpenCobolIDE and pyqode will be worked on in a separate dev branch (that way I can provide hotfix on the master branch). Each hotfix will be integrated into the dev branch, so if you use that branch you will have both the new hot features of v4.7 but also the bugfixes of v4.6.

I think we are mixing up issues here. The original issue mentions Oscar's dependency on `django.contrib.flatpages`. We allow editing of flatpages in the dashboard, and these are used in a few [checkout](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/6ce56ccbc033341a72838b0cb06935d6ba498c75/src/oscar/templates/oscar/partials/nav_checkout.html) [templates ](https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/6ce56ccbc033341a72838b0cb06935d6ba498c75/src/oscar/templates/oscar/checkout/layout.html).

As far as I can see there is no long between `oscar.promotions` and `flatpages`.

That being the case there are two distinct questions here:

1. Do we drop the requirement for `django.contrib.flatpages` and remove flatpage editing from the dashboard? There is

2. Do we drop the promotions app?

Both of these would be breaking changes so it would be good to get a sense of how many projects rely on either feature at present.

There is no easy fix here - in order to display warning message we need to compare current product price and basket product price. 

Since we want to timely inform customer about the actual price change, if the message is not permanent, we need to display it each time price increases or decreases. We can achieve this by saving current product price in the basket line model and compare with the actual product price and only then display message.

Agreed on not liking Git Flow.

My understanding of the previous process (which may not reflect what was done) was:
- New code goes to `develop`
- Releases are tagged on develop occasionally
- `stable` always matches the current released tag (and only exists for user convenience, really)

There's no extra merging or anything between the branches. If a patch release is required on an old version (such as `release-1.5`) a dedicated/temporary branch is made from that tag.

(and the branch names `develop` and `stable` are usually used because `master` is ambiguous: some git projects use a `master` branch for stable, others for development. Better to be explicit with the purpose.)

It does not look like #128 will effect this. The extension binary, which we are trying to pass along as package_data, will not get packaged because of the conditional here:

  https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/29c8385814335e91538d76b68578b6c0ab483c1a/setuptools/command/sdist.py#L147

has_pure_modules() for distutils in Python 3.5.2 is:

```
    def has_pure_modules(self):
        return len(self.packages or self.py_modules or []) > 0
```

There does not appear to be any changes in #128 that change self.packages or self.py_modules.

I pushed my latest commit. I reverted the examples, that did not need the alternative figure creation code, and the ones that did not benefit significantly from axes sharing (using my personal judgment). The following examples kept the alternative syntax:

plot_log_gamma.py - useful to look at details after gamma correction
plot_otsu.py - useful to compare how details are segmented
plot_template.py -useful to look at the exact location of the maximum
plot_equalize.py - useful to look at details after equalization
plot_local_equalize.py - useful to look at details after equalization
plot_register_translation.py - useful to manually inspect translation
plot_ihc_color_separation.py - good example of cross figure axes sharing
plot_adapt_rgb.py - good example of cross figure axes sharing 

I've might have missed some. I've actually missed the last one before commiting. The last two have minimal benefit from axis sharing, but are a good example of cross-figure axes sharing. I would revert only one of them to the preferred figure creation code (the last one).

Hello @kootenpv ! Thanks for the negative feedback, but we'd strongly encourage you to approach this issue in a constructive way as well. There is continued activity here, but after working heavily with graphene over the last few months, it seems like the right way to do this is to use middleware.

If you want permissions on your models (`ObjectType`s) then why not just subclass `ObjectTypeOptions` and `ObjectType` (specifically `__init_subclass_with_meta__`) and add that as an attribute? Then you'll just have to watch for those objects to come by in middleware (usually watching on `info.return_type.graphene_type`.

Or for a more complex authorization architecture, don't define permissions as strings, but add a callable (class, function, or instance) that receives `root` and `info` (which contains your `context`) and make determinations there.

@jkimbo using the value breaks the graphql enum spec. Introspection doesn't provide the value of an enum, only the name. This means that the value is never used once it gets to the client side. By setting the default_value to the enum value, the default_value becomes invalid as input for the enum on the client. This is an oversight of the spec in my opinion but graphql-js implements enum such that serializing the enum grabs the name instead of the value. graphene is not consistent with graphql-js which is considered the standard implementation.

But that is why i tried setting the value to a string that is the same as the name but the parser for default_value for enum's doesn't parse string values correctly for enum. it includes escaped quotes in the value.

On 1 February 2017 at 17:11, Merlijn Sebrechts <notifications@github.com>
wrote:

> The more I think of it, the more I notice that the @preflight hook won't
> solve all possible occurrences of this issue. Let's look at the example of
> a Puppet layer. Puppet modules have to be installed before a higher layer
> can run puppet apply. At first sight, the @preflight hook makes this
> possible. However, the Puppet layer uses the apt layer to install Puppet
> itself. So the order we want is
>
>    1. Apt layer configures sources
>    2. Apt layer installs puppet
>    3. Puppet layer installs sources
>    4. Puppet layer installs modules
>    5. top-level layer runs puppet apply.
>
> In order to achieve this, we would need the @preflight handlers to run in
> a specific order.
>
This example only works if, for some reason, the Puppet layer needs to make
use of the apt layer during the setup phase (before the queue of handlers
gets processed). What is much more likely is that it uses
@when('apt.installed.puppet') like any other consumer of the apt layer.

I have not seen this sort of dependency problem. If it did happen, yes,
@preflight cannot help with ordering and the maintainer of the Pupper layer
would need to coordinate with me to work out some other solution. I do not
think we should add any sort of dependency chain to @preflight handlers; if
we need it, I'd instead propose adding a 'priority' to handlers and turning
the arbitrary ordered queue of handlers into a priority queue.



> About your issues:
>
>    - In the snap layer example, you're basically re-implementing the apt
>    layer, so the solution for the apt layer should work for the snap layer too.
>    - Charms that use the apt layer and run apt commands without using
>    your api are broken. If the api doesn't provide for all use-cases then we
>    should look at fixing the api. I'd be happy to take a look at the code to
>    figure this out. Maybe we can do a small sprint at the Summit?
>    - Cory's proposal for adding objects to flags would allow us to create
>    a much better api for the apt layer that eliminates the need for any upper
>    layer to either call apt manually or use the python apt library.
>
> I now think my example with the snap layer is poor and I can't defend it.
The snap layer doesn't use the apt layer because it doesn't want the apt
specific configuration in config.yaml, and because it doesn't want or need
it then I cannot argue that the apt layer needs to make use of this
(non-existent) configuration during a preflight stage.

I had not thought about attaching package data to flags. Yes, there are
some interesting ideas there. Should handlers be reinvoked if the data
attached to their flags changes? Or are they only invoked when the flag is
first raised?


>
>
> I think it's clear that this kind of stuff is hard, but the @preflight
> handler seems to be a hack that is tied very closely to the technical
> details of your issue.
>
> I think that in general, the issue is that we don't have a way to
> instantly invalidate flags when something happens. apt.installed.x has to
> be unset immediately when landscape removes x. apt.sources.configured has
> to be unset immediately when the sources change.
>

I would not say that the @preflight handler is a hack tied very closely to
the technical details of my issue. It is a tool that neatly and easily let
me solve the technical details of several different issues.

Yes, I think in general it comes down to a way to invalidate flags when
something happens. I think @preflight would be a general way to do this,
providing an opportunity to do this between 'something happens' and the
queue of handlers being processed. It does not account for things happening
entirely outside the hook context, so if a cronjob for instance does a
'charms.reactive get_states' then no handlers (including @preflight) would
kick in. The original proposal in this thread actually could work in this
case, as the rules could be processed without needing the overhead of
starting a hook context (and blocking if something else is running in a
hook context).

Ok, looks like the issue happens when:
- client somehow failed to fetch the room alias
- client leaves the room because it thought the room had no alias (was invalid)
- client was the only one in the room, and then it was left empty
- client then tries to re-create or rejoin the room, but can't do either

Related issues:
https://github.com/matrix-org/synapse/issues/1533
https://github.com/matrix-org/synapse/issues/2141
https://github.com/vector-im/riot-web/issues/1194

Possible solutions:
- being more careful when leaving rooms, maybe double/triple-checking if it really has no alias
- check if we really need to leave a room, maybe avoid leaving it at all and just don't listen to it or avoid leaving if we're the only one in the room

Alright. So what I can do in my application is create a StrictSchema for all Schemas to inherit:

```python
class StrictSchema(Schema):
    """Custom Marshmallow Schema with strict=True in Meta"""

    class Meta:
        """Base Meta class with strict=True

        To inherit in child Schema:

        class ChildSchema(ParentSchema):
            class Meta(ParentSchema.Meta):
                ordered = True
        """
        strict = True
```

then use it as a base here

```python
# Create MySchema programmatically knowing the list of fields
MySchema = type(
    'MySchema',
    (StrictSchema,),  # <--- StrictSchema
    {field: ma.fields.Nested(MyEmbeddedSchema)
     for field in FIELDS}
)
```

And if I need to override Meta:

```python

class MySuperSchema(StrictSchema):
    class Meta(StrictSchema.Meta):
        ordered = True

# Create MySchema programmatically knowing the list of fields
MySchema = type(
    'MySchema',
    (MySuperSchema,),
    {field: ma.fields.Nested(MyEmbeddedSchema)
     for field in FIELDS}
)
```

Thanks to you both.

After looking deeper into it, modifying field instances is not an issue across schema instances, because the `Schema` constructor creates a [deep copy of the `declared_fields` class attribute](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/4c2354c/marshmallow/schema.py#L329) for the instance. It still could be an issue for the field state leaking across multiple `dump()` calls with different arguments on the same schema instance.

Not something I am interested in personally, but contributions are welcome. The basic principle of porting to py3 is to 

1) Convert received data to unicode, preferably as soon as possible, but definitely before returning it to users of the library

Right.  Within the config stuff, we typically want our scripts to be repeatable.  So that means always providing an explicit rng rather than let the object make its own from /dev/urandom.  

If the user doesn't specify a seed, then the config layer will make the default rng and pass that in.  So when actually running a script, this should never be a problem.

However, in tests, where you only run a portion of the config functionality, you sometimes need to add `config['rng'] = galsim.BaseDeviate(123)`.  cf. tests in test_config_value.py and test_config_gsobject.py for examples.

OK, I think I actually figured it out.  The issue, I think, is that our ```_makeSingleGalaxy``` results are not single objects like Sersics, but rather transformations (due to the intrinsic shape, flux rescaling, etc.).  So if we add an ```index``` attribute, then when we do any later transformations to apply the lensing shear, it doesn't get propagated through; instead, the original ```original``` attribute (which was index-free) got propagated.  I think I fixed this in https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/commit/2697f70e4c4003df8d456efff1e79e7b7cd1796e, but the question is whether there are other places where this is also relevant?

I think it should be fine to give them essentially infinity values.  Like 1.e100 or something like that.  You could even try a literal infinity (1.0/0.0) for both and see if anything breaks.  It might work (although math with inf is sometimes problematic).  

Then if this is used as part of a convolution (as it should always be of course), these value will be ignored in the calculation of the net stepk and maxk of the convolution.

Then it would probably also be worth checking what happens with that when someone tries to draw a DeltaFunction without convolving it and make sure that the error message is something comprehensible.  We might need to add a check or two in various places to make sure the user gets the information they need to figure out what they did wrong.

> Is one a strict superset of the other? 

@lukesneeringer By inspection of the documented values at point-of-last-reading, yes:  however, the docs don't *specify* that:  they just describe the two sets which happen to overlap:

- The [`Bucket` resource spec](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/buckets#storageClass) lists all the values currently laid out in our `Bucket._STORAGE_CLASSES` member.
- The [`Object` resource spec](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/objects#storageClass) doesn't list any values at all (boo, hiss!)
- The [Storage Classes concept doc](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes) lists only four (multi-regional, regional, nearline, and coldline), and [defines `STANDARD`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes#standard) as an alias based on the bucket's location type.  It [documents DRA](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes#dra) as a deprecated version, pushing use of multi-regional or regional instead.
- The [Per-Object Storage Class concept docs](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/per-object-storage-class) *only* allow for the four types: I found by inspection that `STANDARD` is settable, and is the value which a new object shows in its resource if the bucket specified no storage class.

We could perhaps coalesce the two lists if we dropped support for setting the DRA type on buckets, or if we could get the storage back-end folks to document the possible values definitively for both buckets and objects.

Also note that (a) https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/fluent-plugin-google-cloud/pull/146 allows enabling JSON detection in any environment, and (b) the fluentd configuration is now decoupled from the contents of the `fluentd-gcp` container image, so can be adjusted at deployment time...

Just to clarify, the `timeout` parameter on your last `run_sync_query` example does not set a timeout for the query. It sets a timeout on waiting for the results. If the request times out, the query is still running in the background. You can verify this by looking at the list of jobs created.

You have to run `QueryJob.cancel()` to actually stop the query, and even that is only best-effort because the query could theoretically complete before the cancel request propagates to the query workers.

One reason for the behavior you are experiencing is that the `result` timeout is in seconds and the provided query will almost certainly complete within 1 second. See: https://docs.python.org/3/library/concurrent.futures.html#concurrent.futures.Future.result

In most cases I just added the new one, so there are lots of questions with both `google-cloud-python` and `gcloud-python`. As for going with `google-cloud-platform` as the tag, it's your call but there are a few issues I see on first impression:

- We can't **make** people use that combo. People will start typing the name of the library in the tag UI and wait until they see it autocomplete. When they post a question on SO, they (with 99% certainty) won't have any idea what our blessed way to tag is.
- `google-cloud-platform` + `python` will be much broader than this library, e.g. most of the `gcloud` CLI is written in Python, GAE has a Python runtime, many other teams use Python data libraries like `pandas`

If you want to use git directly, you have to pass each argument individually. The call you want would be
```python
git.Git().clone(clone_url, to_directory, depth=1)
```

Besides, I'd recommend not to use the git command directly unless you have to. A more pythonic way to achieve the same would be this:

```python
repo = git.Repo.clone_from(clone_url, to_directory, depth=1)
```

`repo` is the hub from which you can do pretty much everything - have a look [at the docs](http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#meet-the-repo-type) for much more information.

I think you got the general idea of it: most tests check if the expected javascript/html is indeed in the rendered output. The current popup tests only check the inside html of the popup. Most other tests render a complete map and check if an expected snippet is included. 

Here is a suggestion. I tried to render the popup, so the output can be compared with an expected string. Note that it needs a parent, that's why I added a feature group (can be anything).
```
def test_popup_sticky():
    fg = FeatureGroup()
    popup = Popup('Some text.', sticky=True).add_to(fg)
    rendered = popup._template.render(this=popup, kwargs={})
    expected = u"""some javascript with the same name inserted""".format(popup.get_name())
    assert rendered == expected
```
